title: Type B Uncertainty: Standard Uncertainty from Prior Knowledge
summary: The GUM splits every uncertainty contribution into two families by *how it is evaluated*: **Type A** (statistical analysis of repeated observations) and **Type B** (any other means — a certificate, a handbook value, a resolution, an interval from experience). Both end as a **standard uncertainty** $u$, an (equivalent) standard deviation. This lesson teaches how to turn a known variability interval $\pm a$ into $u$: rectangular/uniform $u=a/\sqrt3$, U-shaped/arcsine $u=a/\sqrt2$, triangular $u=a/\sqrt6$, and a quoted expanded value $u=U/k$; how to read a bias, a tolerance, or a resolution; and what degrees of freedom to assign a Type B contribution.
objectives: State the GUM definitions of Type A and Type B evaluation and explain that the classification refers to the *method*, not to whether the error is random or systematic.
Recognise that both Type A and Type B contributions are expressed as a standard uncertainty $u$ (an equivalent standard deviation).
Convert a known variability interval $\pm a$ into a standard uncertainty with the right divisor: rectangular $a/\sqrt3$, arcsine (U-shaped) $a/\sqrt2$, triangular $a/\sqrt6$.
Convert a quoted bias / tolerance / accuracy class to a half-width $a$ and then to $u^2=a^2/3$ (rectangular by default).
Treat an instrument resolution $r$ as a rectangular error of half-width $a=r/2$, giving $u=(r/2)/\sqrt3$.
Convert a quoted *expanded* uncertainty back to a standard one with $u=U/k$, recovering $k$ from the Student-$t$ when only the confidence and the d.o.f. are given.
Assign conventional degrees of freedom to a Type B contribution (100 / 30 / 15 by reliability; recommended $\infty$ by GUM).

blocks.text: 1. Why a second way of evaluating uncertainty

The statistical procedure of the previous lessons — take a **sample**, compute its **average** and **variance**, use them as estimates of the population — has two heavy limitations:

- it needs a **large number** of experimental data to give adequate estimates;
- the sample must be **representative**, i.e. it must contain information on *all* the factors that affect the result.

This makes a purely statistical approach **expensive** and **limited to experts**. To overcome this, the *Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement* (**GUM**) introduces a scheme based on **two different types of evaluation** of uncertainty.

An uncertainty contribution evaluated by the **statistical analysis of repeated observations**. You compute a sample standard deviation $s$; if the reported result is the **average** of $n$ readings, its standard uncertainty is $u=s/\sqrt n$ with $\nu=n-1$ degrees of freedom. (GUM: “those evaluated using statistical methods”.)

An uncertainty contribution evaluated by **any other means**: prior knowledge in the technical-scientific literature, a calibration certificate, an instrument resolution, an accuracy class, a tolerance standard, or an interval estimated from experience. (GUM: “those evaluated by other methods”.) You *assume* a probability distribution over the variability interval and derive its equivalent variance.

blocks.title: Both end as a standard uncertainty u
blocks.text: In **both** cases the aim is the **variance** (and hence the standard deviation) of the factor. Type A and Type B contributions are alike called **standard uncertainties**, symbol $u$. The whole point of Type B is to produce an **equivalent standard deviation** so that everything can later be combined with the same statistical machinery.

blocks.title: Type A/B is NOT random vs systematic
blocks.text: The two types refer **only to the method used for the evaluation**, *not* to the object of the measurement and *not* to whether the error is random or systematic. (A systematic bias can be evaluated as Type A from a calibration, or as Type B from an accuracy class.) The link “random = Type A, systematic = Type B” is a common exam trap. The GUM only notes that random effects are *mostly* — not always — evaluated as Type A.

blocks.title: Neither type is automatically better
blocks.text: GUM E.4.3: *evaluations based on repeated observations are not necessarily superior to those obtained by other means.* When the number of observations is limited, a Type B component may be **better known** than a Type A one. The same factor can be obtained either way: replicate the measurement and compute a variance (Type A), or take the value from previous experience (Type B).
2. Two everyday sources of a Type B variability

Two cases recur so often that they deserve a rule of thumb.

**(1) A number given without any stated variability.** If a value is quoted with no tolerance or uncertainty, assume it is *written correctly*: the **number of significant digits** is representative of the variability, which therefore affects only the **least significant digit**. For technical instruments it is reasonable to take a variability of **$\pm 2$ units of the least significant digit**.

**(2) A declared instrument bias.** The bias may come (a) from a **calibration certificate** giving the input–output characteristic and its uncertainty (then you *correct* the reading and the residual is Type A), or (b) only as a **declaration of conformity** to a standard or an **accuracy class** — then you know only a *variability interval* in which the bias can fall, and you must do a **Type B** evaluation.

blocks.title: Micrometer with a declaration of conformity
blocks.text: A micrometer with a $25$ mm range, declared conforming to ISO 3611:2010, may have a **maximum error anywhere in $\pm 4\ \mu$m**. This interval does **not** describe the actual spread of repeated readings — it describes the spread of the *characteristics of this type of instrument*. With no information on where inside $\pm 4\ \mu$m the bias sits, you treat it as a Type B contribution with half-width $a=4\ \mu$m and a uniform distribution, so $u=a/\sqrt3=4/\sqrt3\approx 2.31\ \mu$m.
3. From an interval ±a to a standard uncertainty: three distributions

Type B information almost always arrives as a **variability interval** $\pm a$. We must turn it into an **equivalent variance** — the variance of the distribution of the factor inside that range. Because uncertainty is only known to a coarse approximation, only **three** shapes are used: **uniform (rectangular)**, **arcsine (U-shaped)** and **triangular**. Each gives a different divisor of the half-width $a$.

Adopted when **nothing is known** about the behaviour of the factor inside its range $\pm a$ — every value is equally likely. This is the **default**, and the one used for **reading resolution**, **handbook/tabled coefficients**, and **accuracy classes**.

blocks.caption: Equivalent variance of a uniform distribution of half-width $a$. Derived from $\sigma^2=\int_{-a}^{a}(x-\mu)^2\frac{1}{2a}\,dx=\frac{a^2}{3}$ (with $\mu=0$ at the centre).

blocks.text: Adopted for factors of **sinusoidal type** that linger near their extremes — e.g. the natural day–night swing of an **unconditioned** temperature, vibrations, oscillations. Random sampling in time lands more often near the maximum/minimum than near the centre, so the probability density is **higher at the edges** (U-shaped). With $\theta=a\sin(2\pi t/T)$ one finds $P(\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\pi\sqrt{a^2-\theta^2}}$.

blocks.caption: Equivalent variance of the arcsine (U-shaped) distribution. The piling-up of probability at the extremes makes this variance **larger** than the rectangular one for the same $a$ (divisor $\sqrt2$, not $\sqrt3$). In an uncertainty table this corresponds to the divisor $k_a=2$.

blocks.text: A third option — **not recommended for inexpert users** — adopted when, while knowing little, it is reasonable to suppose the **central value is more likely** than the edges. The density is a symmetric triangle over $\pm a$.

blocks.caption: Equivalent variance of the triangular distribution of half-width $a$. Because probability is concentrated in the centre, this is the **smallest** of the three variances for the same $a$ ($\sqrt6>\sqrt3>\sqrt2$).

blocks.title: The divisor table — memorise it
blocks.text:
For a Type B input with half-width $a$:
- **Rectangular / uniform**: $u=a/\sqrt3$  (divisor factor $k_a=3$ on the variance) — the default, and for resolution and tabled values.
- **Arcsine / U-shaped**: $u=a/\sqrt2$  ($k_a=2$) — sinusoidal, unconditioned-temperature effects.
- **Triangular**: $u=a/\sqrt6$  ($k_a=6$) — when the centre is more likely.

Largest $u$ first: arcsine $>$ rectangular $>$ triangular, for the same $a$.
4. Resolution: half a step, rectangular

The reading **resolution** $r$ is the archetypal rectangular Type B contribution. Suppose an analogue scale outputs a natural number. If you read “1”, the true measurand may lie **anywhere between 0.5 and 1.5**, with no reason to prefer any value — a uniform distribution. The **half-width** of that interval is $a=r/2$.

blocks.caption: Standard uncertainty from a resolution $r$. Mind the half-step: the variability spans one resolution step $r$, so the half-width is $r/2$ — not $r$.

blocks.title: Std-dev vs variance vs expanded vs half-width

blocks.text: 5. From a quoted expanded uncertainty back to a standard one

Calibration certificates usually quote an **expanded uncertainty** $U$ (a confidence half-width), not a standard one. Since $U=k\,u$, recovering the standard uncertainty needed for a budget is just a division:

blocks.caption: Recover the standard uncertainty from an expanded one. If the certificate gives a confidence level $P$ and degrees of freedom $\nu$, the factor is the Student-$t$ quantile. If it only says “$2\sigma$” / 95% with many d.o.f., use $k=2$.

blocks.title: A certificate bias with few measurements
blocks.text: A certificate gives a bias as **$U=3.7\ \mu$m at 95% confidence, obtained on 4 measurements**. With $n=4$, the degrees of freedom are $\nu=n-1=3$, so the factor is the Student-$t$ quantile $k=t_{0.975,3}=$ T.INV$(0.975,3)=3.18$ — **not** the asymptotic $1.96$. Hence $u=3.7/3.18\approx1.16\ \mu$m. Using $1.96$ here would understate the standard uncertainty.
6. Degrees of freedom for a Type B contribution

Degrees of freedom measure the **reliability** of an uncertainty contribution: a Type A average has $
u=n-1$; a regression slope has $
u=n-n_p$ (data minus fitted parameters). Type B values carry no sample size, yet we still need a $
u$ for them, because the combination step (Welch–Satterthwaite) needs one per contribution.

Type B contributions are usually very **robust**: the GUM nominally recommends $
u=\infty$. In practice it is reasonable to use a **finite** value reflecting how consolidated the information is.

blocks.title: Conventional Type B degrees of freedom

question.prompt: According to the GUM, what distinguishes a **Type A** from a **Type B** uncertainty contribution?

options.content: Type A is for random errors, Type B is for systematic errors
Type A is evaluated by statistical analysis of repeated observations; Type B by any other means
Type A is always larger than Type B
Type A applies to the instrument, Type B to the measurand

question.explanation: The classification is purely about the **method of evaluation**: statistical (Type A) vs any other means such as certificates, resolution, handbooks, experience (Type B). **A** is the classic trap — the types are explicitly *not* tied to random/systematic (random effects are only *mostly* Type A). **C** is false: GUM E.4.3 says neither is automatically superior. **D** invents a distinction; both refer to the evaluation method, not the object.
question.theory: Type A = statistical analysis of repeated observations; Type B = any other means. Both produce a standard uncertainty $u$.

practice.prompt: An instrument's **reading resolution** is $r$. What standard uncertainty does it contribute, and from which distribution?

options.content: $u=r$ — the resolution is itself the standard uncertainty

practice.explanation: A reading can fall anywhere within one resolution step, so the error is **rectangular** with **half-width $a=r/2$**, giving $u=a/\sqrt3=(r/2)/\sqrt3$. **A** uses the full step as the half-width and double-counts the spread. **C** picks the wrong divisor — triangular is for a peaked, not a flat, density. **D** treats a half-width as a standard deviation, far too large.

practice.prompt: Which divisor turns a **triangular** half-width $a$ into a standard uncertainty?
practice.explanation: A symmetric triangular distribution of half-width $a$ has variance $a^2/6$, so $u=a/\sqrt6$. **D** ($\sqrt3$) is the **rectangular** divisor — a flatter density spreads more probability to the edges, giving a larger $u$. **A** ($\sqrt2$) is the **arcsine/U-shaped** divisor. **B** is the *variance* factor $k_a$ of the arcsine case, not a standard-uncertainty divisor.
practice.theory: Divisors of the half-width: rectangular $\sqrt3$, arcsine $\sqrt2$, triangular $\sqrt6$.

practice.prompt: Measurements are taken at random times over many days in a room **without temperature control**, where the temperature swings sinusoidally by $\pm a$. Which distribution and standard uncertainty apply to the temperature?
practice.explanation: A sinusoidal swing lingers near its extremes, so random-in-time sampling is **more likely** to catch the maximum/minimum — a **U-shaped (arcsine)** density, $u=a/\sqrt2$. **B** (rectangular) would apply in a *conditioned* room where the trend is roughly flat across the band. **C** (triangular) assumes the centre is most likely — the opposite of a sinusoid. **D** invents a normal model with the wrong divisor.
practice.theory: Unconditioned, sinusoidal day–night temperature → arcsine, $u^2=a^2/2$. Conditioned → rectangular, $u^2=a^2/3$.

practice.prompt: A calibration certificate gives a bias as **expanded uncertainty $U=3.7\ \mu$m at 95% confidence, on 4 measurements**. How do you obtain the standard uncertainty for the budget?
practice.explanation: An *expanded* uncertainty is divided by its coverage factor, $u=U/k$. With only $n=4$ measurements the d.o.f. are $\nu=3$, so $k$ is the Student-$t$ quantile $t_{0.975,3}=3.18$. **A** wrongly treats a certificate's expanded value as a rectangular half-width. **B** forgets to remove the coverage. **D** uses $1.96$, valid only for infinite d.o.f.; with $\nu=3$ it badly understates $k$ (and thus inflates $u$ would be wrong direction — it understates $u$).
practice.theory: Expanded → standard: $u=U/k$, with $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}$ from T.INV when the certificate states a confidence and d.o.f.

practice.prompt: A micrometer declared conforming to a standard may have its maximum error **anywhere in $\pm 4\ \mu$m**. No further information is available. The standard uncertainty of this bias is closest to:
practice.explanation: “Anywhere in $\pm 4\ \mu$m” with nothing else known → **rectangular**, half-width $a=4\ \mu$m, so $u=a/\sqrt3=4/1.732=2.31\ \mu$m. **A** treats the half-width as the standard uncertainty (no divisor). **C** uses the arcsine divisor $\sqrt2$ ($4/\sqrt2=2.83$), wrong unless the cause is sinusoidal. **D** uses the triangular divisor $\sqrt6$ ($4/\sqrt6=1.63$), only valid if the centre were more likely.
practice.theory: A declaration of conformity / accuracy class gives a variability interval → default rectangular, $u=a/\sqrt3$.

practice.prompt: A thermal-expansion coefficient is reported in a handbook as $\alpha=25\times10^{-6}\ ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, with no stated tolerance. By the significant-digits convention, what variance $u^2(\alpha)$ do you assign?
practice.explanation: With no tolerance, assume the value is written correctly: variability is $\pm2$ units of the **least significant digit**, i.e. $a=2\times10^{-6}\ ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, and a tabled value is taken **rectangular**, so $u^2=a^2/3=(2\times10^{-6})^2/3=1.3\times10^{-12}$. **A** uses the right $a$ but a triangular divisor. **B** uses 1 unit instead of 2 of the least significant digit. **D** wrongly assumes a noiseless value — a number always carries the variability of its last digit.
practice.theory: Number with no stated variability → $a=2$ units of the least significant digit, rectangular: $u^2=a^2/3$.

practice.prompt: For the same half-width $a$, rank the standard uncertainties produced by the rectangular (R), arcsine/U-shaped (U) and triangular (T) distributions, from largest to smallest.
practice.explanation: The variances are $a^2/2$ (U), $a^2/3$ (R), $a^2/6$ (T), so $u_U=a/\sqrt2 > u_R=a/\sqrt3 > u_T=a/\sqrt6$. The U-shape piles probability at the extremes (largest spread); the triangle concentrates it at the centre (smallest). **A** and **B** scramble the order. **C** is false — the three divisors $\sqrt2,\sqrt3,\sqrt6$ are different.

practice.prompt: An input is described by a measured **standard deviation** $s=3\ \mu$m of a stability test. How does it enter the budget as a standard uncertainty?

options.content: $u=3\ \mu$m — a standard deviation is already a standard uncertainty

practice.explanation: A **standard deviation is itself a standard uncertainty** — no divisor, no square root, no coverage division: $u=s=3\ \mu$m (divisor 1). **B** wrongly applies a rectangular divisor meant for a half-width interval. **C** would take $\sqrt{s}$, which is dimensionally nonsense here. **D** confuses $s$ with an expanded uncertainty (which would need $u=U/k$).
practice.theory: Standard deviation → $u=s$ (divisor 1); variance → $u=\sqrt V$; expanded → $u=U/k$; half-width → $u=a/\sqrt{k_a}$.

practice.prompt: A Type B contribution is judged **consolidated** (reliable, but not extremely so). What conventional number of degrees of freedom is assigned to it?
practice.explanation:
The conventional Type B scale is $
u=100$ (extremely consolidated), $
u=30$ (consolidated), $
u=15$ (to be checked). “Consolidated” → **$
u=30$**. **A** ($15$) is for information *to be checked*. **B** ($100$) is for *extremely consolidated*. **C** ($1$) is far too low — Type B values are robust; GUM even nominally recommends $\infty$.
practice.theory: Conventional Type B d.o.f.: 100 / 30 / 15 by reliability (extremely consolidated / consolidated / to be checked).

practice.prompt: Which statement about Type A and Type B evaluations is **correct** according to the GUM?

options.content: The same factor can be evaluated either as Type A (replicate and compute the variance) or as Type B (take it from previous experience).
A Type B component can never be better known than a Type A one.
Type B evaluations are forbidden once any experimental data exist.
Type A and Type B contributions are combined by different formulas.

practice.explanation: GUM stresses the *same factor* may be handled either way: replicate it for a Type A variance, or import it from experience as Type B. **B** contradicts E.4.3, which says a Type B component may be *better known* when observations are few. **C** is false — Type B is precisely useful in planning, before or alongside data. **D** is false: once each is a standard uncertainty $u$, both feed the **same** propagation-of-variance law.
practice.theory: Type A and Type B differ only in evaluation method; both yield $u$ and combine identically. Neither is automatically superior.
